questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Identify the correct statements from the following.
options: The luminous efficiency of a monochromatic source with a wavelength of $555 \text{ nm}$ always exceeds that of a white light source having the same power.
The illuminating power of a monochromatic source with a wavelength of $555 \text{ nm}$ always exceeds that of a white light source having the same power.
The luminous efficiency of a monochromatic source always exceeds that of a white light source having the same power.
The illuminating power of a monochromatic source always exceeds that of a white light source having the same power.
questions.solution:
The relative luminosity of the human eye is maximum at a wavelength of $555 \text{ nm}$.

Luminous efficiency is the ratio of total luminous flux to the total radiant power. For a monochromatic source of wavelength $555 \text{ nm}$, the luminous efficiency is maximum. A white light source contains various wavelengths across the visible spectrum, for most of which the relative luminosity is less than $1$. Thus, the overall luminous efficiency of a white light source is less than that of a monochromatic source at $555 \text{ nm}$.

Illuminating power is proportional to the luminous flux. Since the luminous efficiency of the $555 \text{ nm}$ source is higher, its luminous flux and illuminating power will also be greater than those of a white light source of the same radiant power.

A monochromatic source at a different wavelength (e.g., $700 \text{ nm}$) has a very low luminous efficiency, which can be lower than that of a white light source. Thus, statements (3) and (4) are incorrect.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Select the correct options from the following.
options: Relative luminosity is a quantity without dimensions.
Radiant flux and power possess identical dimensions.
Luminous flux and radiant flux possess identical dimensions.
Luminous flux and luminous intensity possess identical dimensions.
questions.solution:
Relative luminosity is defined as the ratio of the luminosity of a particular wavelength to the maximum luminosity. Being a ratio of two identical quantities, it is a dimensionless quantity.

Radiant flux is the total radiant energy emitted per unit time. Its unit is Watt, which is the same as that of power. Thus, radiant flux and power have identical dimensions of $[M L^2 T^{-3}]$.

Luminous flux is measured in lumens, where $1 \text{ lumen} = 1 \text{ candela} \times 1 \text{ steradian}$. Since solid angle (steradian) is a dimensionless quantity, luminous flux has the same dimensions as luminous intensity, which is $[J]$.

Radiant flux has dimensions of $[M L^2 T^{-3}]$, while luminous flux has dimensions of $[J]$. Hence, they do not possess identical dimensions.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An extended source illuminates a room. The illuminance at a specific section of a wall can be enhanced by
options: decreasing the luminous flux of the source
placing certain mirrors in suitable locations
questions.solution:
Illuminance at a surface is the luminous flux incident per unit area. It depends on the distance from the source, the orientation of the source (for an extended source), and the total flux reaching the surface.

Shifting the source closer to the wall decreases the distance, thereby increasing the illuminance.

Rotating an extended source can change the luminous intensity directed towards the specific section of the wall, which can enhance the illuminance if oriented optimally.

Decreasing the luminous flux of the source will reduce the illuminance.

Placing mirrors in suitable locations can direct additional reflected light onto the specific section of the wall, thereby increasing the total luminous flux incident on it and enhancing the illuminance.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The brightness-producing capability of a source
options: is independent of the emitted wavelength
is dependent on the emitted wavelength
questions.solution:
The brightness-producing capability of a light source is determined by its luminous flux, which measures the perceived power of light.

The human eye is not equally sensitive to all wavelengths of light. The sensitivity is maximum for a wavelength of $555 \text{ nm}$ (yellow-green region) and decreases for other wavelengths. Thus, the brightness depends on the emitted wavelength.

Additionally, for a specific wavelength, a source emitting more radiant energy per unit time (radiant power) will produce a greater visual sensation. Thus, the brightness also depends on the power of the source.

Therefore, the brightness-producing capability is dependent on both the emitted wavelength and its power.
